behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old presents with strabismus due to epicanthus. Which is correct?

options.A: It must be corrected before amblyopia develops
options.D: Improves by covering the strong eye
options.E: It is a form of cohesive strabismus

questions.explanation: Epicanthus causes pseudostrabismus — no true deviation, so no correction is needed.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old reports images appear crumpled in the centre. Metamorphopsia:

options.A: Is diagnosed with an Amsler grid
options.B: Is often seen in optic nerve disease

questions.explanation: Metamorphopsia is a macular symptom, screened with the Amsler grid.

questions.stem: A newborn has mucopurulent discharge days after birth (neonatal ophthalmia). Which is correct?

options.A: May be due to gonococcus, chlamydia, herpes or other agents
options.B: Occurs on the day of delivery

questions.explanation: Multiple infectious agents can cause it; it needs treatment and is usually bilateral.

questions.stem: Which disease causes acute stabbing eye pain that worsens with blinking?
questions.explanation: A raw corneal surface (UV photokeratitis) hurts sharply on each blink.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old has reduced vision for a week with right RAPD. It may be due to:
questions.explanation: Subacute monocular loss with an RAPD points to optic (retrobulbar) neuritis.

questions.stem: Pathological cupping of the optic disc is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Progressive cupping is the structural hallmark of chronic glaucoma.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old myope has 24 h of floaters and photopsias. This may indicate:
questions.explanation: Floaters + flashes in a myope = posterior vitreous detachment with risk of retinal detachment — must exclude RD.

questions.stem: Retinoblastoma:

options.A: Is the most common ocular tumour in humans

questions.explanation: Leukocoria is the classic sign; it is malignant, usually under age 2, with familial cases (RB1).

questions.stem: An 82-year-old with headache, sudden vision loss, disc oedema, weight loss and malaise. Likely:

options.C: Ischemic optic neuropathy due to giant cell arteritis

questions.explanation: Classic GCA (Horton's) → AION. Needs urgent steroids.

questions.stem: The most common intraocular tumour in adults is:
questions.explanation: Choroidal (uveal) melanoma is the commonest primary intraocular tumour in adults.

questions.stem: In oculomotor (CN III) nerve palsy, one may observe:
questions.explanation: CN III palsy → mydriasis (and ptosis, down-and-out eye). Lagophthalmos is CN VII.

questions.stem: Regarding corneal epithelial shedding / corneal ulcer:

options.B: Affects only the epithelium, never deeper layers

questions.explanation: Fluorescein under cobalt-blue light reveals the epithelial defect.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old with pain on eye movements most likely has:
questions.explanation: Pain on eye movement is the hallmark of retrobulbar optic neuritis, often MS-related.

questions.stem: Which is true for scleritis?

options.A: It is not characterized by deep pain
options.C: In severe form it is associated with scleral perforation

questions.explanation: Scleritis: deep pain, does NOT blanch with phenylephrine, needs steroids, can perforate.

questions.stem: An infant with high hyperopia is likely to develop:
questions.explanation: Excess accommodation to overcome hyperopia drives convergence → accommodative esotropia.

questions.stem: Damage to the right occipital lobe causes:
questions.explanation: Right visual cortex lesion → left homonymous hemianopia.

questions.stem: During the cover-uncover test in a patient with exophoria:

options.A: The covered eye does not move
options.B: The uncovered eye does not move

questions.explanation: Covering breaks fusion → the covered eye drifts outward (its latent exo position); it re-fixates inward on uncovering.

questions.stem: Which corneal layer mainly maintains transparency?
questions.explanation: The endothelial Na⁺/K⁺ pump keeps the stroma dehydrated and clear.

questions.stem: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is characterized by:

options.B: Sharp focal pain on blinking and tearing
options.D: Near vision loss with watery discharge
options.E: Conjunctival hyperemia, corneal haze and a mid-dilated pupil

questions.explanation: Classic AACG: red eye, hazy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, very high IOP.

questions.stem: A welder comes home in the evening with severe eye pain and tearing. Most likely:

options.A: No treatment needed, recovers by morning
options.B: UV radiation burn with punctate keratitis
options.D: Treat with topical anesthetic for 2 days

questions.explanation: UV photokeratitis with punctate epithelial keratitis. Anaesthetic abuse is harmful.

questions.stem: Which disease requires immediate therapeutic intervention?
questions.explanation: Accelerated/malignant hypertensive retinopathy (with disc swelling) demands urgent BP control.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old develops postoperative right facial nerve palsy. Which is correct?

options.A: Correcting the drooping brow is the priority
options.B: VA is reduced from paralysis of accommodation
options.C: It paralyses the levator, pupil sphincter and masseter
options.D: Priority is corneal protection from lagophthalmos with lubricants
options.E: Bell's phenomenon doesn't affect exposure keratitis

questions.explanation: CN VII palsy → lagophthalmos → protect the cornea with lubricants/ointment first.

questions.stem: Which is true for refractive errors?

options.A: Effortless distance focus on the retina can coexist with presbyopia
options.C: Myopia is corrected with biconvex cylindrical lenses
options.D: Myopes need higher presbyopic correction than emmetropes
options.E: Presbyopia is corrected with diverging lenses

questions.explanation: An emmetrope (effortless distance focus) still develops presbyopia with age.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old develops an inflamed chalazion. Which is correct?

options.A: Soft-tissue inflammation needing immediate incision in all cases
options.B: Infected accessory sebaceous gland needing systemic antibiotics
options.D: Meibomian gland infection, treated initially with warm compresses and antibiotic+steroid ointment
options.E: Larger version of a stye, treated only with compresses and systemic antibiotics

questions.explanation: An inflamed chalazion (meibomian gland) is managed initially with warm compresses and antibiotic±steroid ointment.

questions.stem: Which is true for episcleritis?
questions.explanation:
Episcleritis is usually idiopathic, mild and self-limiting.

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): Debated: 'blanches with phenylephrine' (option e) is also a true feature — the printed stem was incomplete, so either may be the intended key.

questions.stem: Which is correct for a 78-year-old with senile cataract?

options.A: Accompanied by deep pain and redness
options.B: A hypermature cataract never causes phacomorphic glaucoma
options.D: Vision always restored with corrective lenses
options.E: Progressive painless decrease in visual acuity

questions.explanation: Senile cataract = gradual painless VA loss. Hypermature lenses CAN cause phacomorphic glaucoma.

questions.stem: A mydriatic is contraindicated in:
questions.explanation: Dilating the pupil can precipitate/worsen angle closure.

questions.stem: Which is correct about visual fields?

options.A: A positive scotoma example is ischemic optic neuropathy blindness
options.B: Small peripheral arcuate loss is typical of vitreous detachment
options.C: Bilateral central scotoma can be due to occipital lobe damage
options.D: In bitemporal hemianopia, scotomas affect the temporal field of each eye
options.E: Vertical homonymous hemianopia can be due to retinal vascular damage

questions.explanation: Chiasmal compression → loss of both temporal fields = bitemporal hemianopia.

questions.stem: The primary function of the ciliary body is:

options.A: Its relaxation loosens lens tension, regulating accommodation
options.C: Its relaxation changes corneal curvature for near vision
options.D: Its epithelium aids drainage of anterior-chamber aqueous

questions.explanation:
The intended answer concerns accommodation.

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): Wording is imprecise: ciliary muscle CONTRACTION (not relaxation) loosens the zonules for near focus. It produces — not drains — aqueous.

questions.stem: Branch retinal artery occlusion:

options.B: Causes sudden reduction in visual acuity
options.C: Is painful and directly affects vision

questions.explanation: BRAO → sudden, painless, sectoral visual loss.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman: chronic foreign-body sensation for months, Schirmer I 3 mm. Most likely:
questions.explanation: Low Schirmer + chronic dryness = aqueous-deficient dry eye, classically Sjögren's.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old with thyroid eye disease — what is NOT true?

options.A: Has reduced IOP due to hyperthyroidism
options.E: Often associated with upper lid retraction

questions.explanation: Thyroid orbitopathy does not lower IOP — orbital congestion tends to raise it.

questions.stem: Glaucoma examination does NOT include:
questions.explanation: Schirmer is a dry-eye test, not part of glaucoma assessment.

questions.stem: Myopia:

options.C: Is corrected with biconvex diverging lenses
options.D: Is corrected with a cylindrical lens

questions.explanation: Myopia is the opposite of hyperopia; corrected with biconcave (diverging) lenses.

questions.stem: Sudden decrease in vision in one eye in a 47-year-old can be due to:

options.B: Acute glaucoma crisis when accompanied by deep pain

questions.explanation: Cataract/presbyopia are gradual; sudden painful loss fits an acute glaucoma crisis.

questions.stem: Which is correct for acute dacryocystitis?

options.A: Due to acute inflammation of the lacrimal gland
options.B: Caused by obstruction at the nasolacrimal duct, needs systemic antibiotics
options.C: Treated with topical antibiotic drops only
options.D: Due to obstruction at the level of the lacrimal canaliculi

questions.explanation: Acute dacryocystitis = infected sac from nasolacrimal duct obstruction → systemic antibiotics.

questions.stem: A person with 5/10 visual acuity:

options.C: Goes shopping only in their neighbourhood

questions.explanation:
Per the exam key, the marked answer is that they cannot comfortably read continuous (small) print.

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): Marked answer from the paper. Clinically debatable, since 5/10 meets many driving thresholds — go with the official key.

questions.stem: Regarding acute glaucoma, which is FALSE?

options.C: It can also affect the second eye

questions.explanation: False statement: in acute angle-closure the cornea is hazy/oedematous, not clear.

questions.stem: Which condition is NOT due to external causes?
questions.explanation: Scleritis is typically associated with systemic autoimmune disease, not an external/infectious cause.

questions.stem: Which is correct for Sjögren's syndrome?

options.A: Requires only local, not systemic, treatment
options.B: Not related to an autoimmune reaction
options.C: Characterized by dry eyes and dry mouth
options.D: The damage is located in the meibomian glands

questions.explanation: Sjögren's: autoimmune destruction of lacrimal & salivary glands → dry eyes and dry mouth.

questions.stem: Safe driving requires a visual field (nasal/temporal/upper/lower) of:
questions.explanation:
Matches roughly normal field extents (nasal 60, temporal ~120 binocular, superior 60, inferior 75).

ⓘ Ambiguity note (flagged by author): Exact required values vary by source/jurisdiction — confirm against your lecture notes.

questions.stem: What is true for asthenopia (eye strain)?
questions.explanation: Asthenopia occurs with heterophoria (latent strabismus) and uncorrected hyperopia; it worsens through the day, not the morning.

questions.stem: Accommodative convergent strabismus in young children requires:
questions.explanation: Correcting the underlying hyperopia with glasses removes the accommodative drive and straightens the eyes.

questions.stem: Pain that worsens through the day and with computer work is due to:

options.A: Inflammation of the iris and ciliary body

questions.explanation: Uncorrected hyperopia → sustained accommodative effort for near work → end-of-day asthenopic pain.

questions.stem: Chronic corneal epithelial shedding from neurotrophic keratopathy:

options.A: Is treated by fitting a contact lens
options.B: Is characterized by deep pain and itching
options.C: Is treated with lubricants and cortisone drops
options.D: Is characterized by reduced corneal sensation

questions.explanation: Neurotrophic keratopathy is defined by reduced corneal sensation — hence little pain despite epithelial breakdown.

questions.stem: In herpes zoster ophthalmicus:

options.B: Treatment is based on topical antibiotics
options.C: Iridocyclitis and ocular hypertension can coexist
options.D: Corneal lesions are always true dendritic ulcers

questions.explanation: HZO is unilateral, treated with antivirals, and frequently has associated iridocyclitis with raised IOP; lesions are pseudodendrites.

questions.stem: A construction worker with lime (calcium) splashed on the ocular surface:

options.A: No concern for long-term eye health
options.B: Pressure dressing + antibiotic ointment for a day, then outpatient review
options.C: Immediate copious irrigation, eyelid eversion and urgent hospital referral

questions.explanation: Alkali burn → immediate, prolonged irrigation, evert lids to remove particles, refer urgently.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old myope with −5D sees clearly at what distance?
questions.explanation: Far point = 1/5 m = 20 cm.

questions.stem: In advanced (proliferative) diabetic retinopathy:

options.A: Vision loss can come from vitreous/retrohyaloid haemorrhage and fibrous membranes on the detached cortical vitreous
options.B: Vision loss is from serous retinal detachment due to massive neovascular leakage into the subretinal space
options.C: Vision loss is from neovessels on the posterior lens surface

questions.explanation: Proliferative DR loses vision via vitreous haemorrhage and tractional fibrovascular membranes.

questions.stem: A possible cause of raised intraocular pressure is:

options.A: Failure of transfer from the posterior chamber to the vitreous
options.C: Reduced drainage through the canal of Schlemm

questions.explanation: Raised IOP usually results from impaired aqueous outflow (trabecular meshwork / canal of Schlemm).
